title: TabFlex: Scaling Tabular Learning to Millions with Linear Attention
authors: Yuchen Zeng, Tuan Dinh, Wonjun Kang, Andreas C. Mueller (Microsoft Gray Systems Lab; UW-Madison; UCSF; Furiosa AI; SNU)
tokenization_method: Inherits TabPFN's sample-as-token in-context scheme: an entire feature vector x (and label y for train samples) is embedded by a shared MLP into ONE token per sample; the dataset becomes a sequence of sample-tokens classified in a single forward pass via in-context learning. TabFlex's own contribution is NOT a new feature tokenizer — it replaces TabPFN's quadratic softmax self-attention with non-causal linear attention (kernel feature map phi = elu(.)+1), keeping order-invariance over training tokens while making attention O(N) in sequence length (number of samples).
feature_types_handled: numerical and categorical, mixed (handled exactly as TabPFN: features concatenated into a fixed-width vector, padded/projected to the sub-model's feature capacity; no temporal handling). Token granularity is per-SAMPLE, not per-feature.
token_granularity: per-sample (one token = one whole row / feature vector). Features are NOT individually tokenized; they are collapsed by a single input-projection MLP.
numerical_strategy: raw scalar values fed into the per-sample input-projection MLP after TabPFN-style preprocessing (normalization); no piecewise-linear / periodic / quantile feature embeddings are introduced. High feature counts (>1000) are reduced by random linear projection before encoding.
categorical_strategy: inherited from TabPFN (categoricals are encoded into the numeric feature vector via TabPFN's preprocessing; the paper introduces no new learned per-category tokens or high-cardinality scheme).
high_cardinality_handling: none specific. High-DIMENSIONALITY (many features, d>1000) is handled by random projection to 1000 features (Alg.1); high-CARDINALITY categoricals are not addressed beyond TabPFN's default preprocessing.
temporal_handling: none (classification over static feature vectors; no time-varying support).
scales_to_many_features: Sub-model TabFlex-H1K trained for up to 1K features and 100 classes; datasets with d>1000 are random-projected down to 1000. Linear attention makes cost linear in the number of SAMPLES (sequence length), not in feature count; feature count still capped at ~1-2K and degrades vs XGBoost above ~800 features. Conditional model selection (Alg.1) routes by (n,d): TabFlex-S100 (n<=3K,d<=100), TabFlex-L100 (n>=3K,d<=100, 50K-length prompts), TabFlex-H1K (d>100 or high d/n).
downstream_model: 12-layer Transformer-style encoder (TabPFN architecture) with softmax attention replaced by non-causal linear attention; final per-test-token MLP produces class probabilities.
pretraining_objective: TabPFN-style prior-fitted-network pretraining: trained offline on large numbers of synthetic datasets sampled from a structural-causal-model / Bayesian prior, learning to do Bayesian in-context inference; no per-dataset fine-tuning at deploy time (training-free adaptation).
key_results: 115 OpenML datasets + TabZilla hard (36 datasets). On 57 small datasets: AUC essentially tied with TabPFN (median 0.96 vs 0.97) at >2x speedup; top-2 overall. On 11 large/high-dim TabZilla-hard datasets: substantially beats TabPFN because it uses ALL training data (TabPFN capped at 3000). poker-hand (1.025M samples): TabFlex AUC 0.84 in 4.88s vs TabPFN 0.72/15.36s and 5th-best baseline 0.54/504s. >2x speedup over TabPFN, ~1.5x over XGBoost; beats 25 baselines on efficiency. Regression handled only by binning the target (10/100 bins) into classification — clearly weaker than XGBoost on most regression datasets.
baselines_compared: TabPFN, TabPFNv2 (discussed as concurrent), TuneTables, HyperFast, XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost, RandomForest, SVM, KNN, DecisionTree, LinearModel, FT-Transformer, TabTransformer, SAINT, TabNet, ResNet, MLP-rtdl, DeepFM, NODE, DANet, STG, VIME, NAM (25 baselines via McElfresh et al. 2023 / TabZilla). Also internal ablations: Mamba-II SSM vs Transformer, causal vs non-causal attention, sliding-window vs linear attention.
transferable_idea: Non-causal linear attention (phi=elu(.)+1) as a drop-in replacement for softmax self-attention in a set-of-rows in-context tabular model, preserving permutation-invariance over context while turning attention cost linear in sequence length — the lever that lets a TabPFN-style foundation model ingest very long contexts (50K+ in-context examples, millions of test rows in one pass). Key empirical finding worth borrowing: CAUSAL sequence models (SSMs/Mamba, causal linear attention) are systematically WORSE for tabular ICL because order-of-examples should not matter; use non-causal.
limitations: (1) Only classification natively; regression is a crude target-binning hack and loses to XGBoost. (2) Feature capacity ~1-2K; above ~800 features XGBoost overtakes it and >1000 features are lossily random-projected. (3) Inherits TabPFN's preprocessing entirely — no advance in numerical/categorical/high-cardinality tokenization. (4) Three separately-trained sub-models + hand-tuned selection thresholds rather than one model. (5) No temporal/mixed time-varying support.
pitfalls: The abstract's 'thousands of features' is a capacity ceiling reached via RANDOM PROJECTION, not faithful per-feature representation — do not read it as evidence that TabFlex tokenizes many features well. 'Scales to millions' refers to SAMPLES (rows), which is orthogonal to this topic's 70+-FEATURE tokenization goal. The paper's contribution is the attention mechanism, not feature tokenization; its feature handling is identical to (and inherits the weaknesses of) TabPFN. Linear-attention 'no accuracy loss' is shown on the 57-dataset suite where TabPFN was already strong; on harder/high-dim data the comparison is confounded by also using more training data.
relevance_to_topic: Tangential-but-instructive for the 70+-mixed-feature build: it shows the SAMPLE-axis scaling lever (linear attention) but contributes nothing to the FEATURE-axis tokenization problem; useful mainly as the efficient-attention backbone you'd pair with a real feature tokenizer, plus the strong negative result that causal models hurt tabular ICL.
code_available: yes - https://github.com/microsoft/ticl (Microsoft TICL repo; same codebase family as TabPFN)
